Title: Beyond the Toggle: Feature Flags as Your Cloud-Native Migration Safety Net
Abstract:
Cloud migrations, API rewrites, and architecture transformations don’t usually fail because of bad code; they fail because teams rely on “big bang” releases that concentrate risk into a single moment.

More than half of large-scale transformations never fully deliver on their intended outcomes. The difference between failure and success isn’t the migration itself; it’s how you release it.

In this talk, we’ll explore how modern engineering teams use feature flags and experimentation as a cloud-native safety net, decoupling deployment from release to enable gradual, reversible, and data-driven migrations.

You’ll learn how to:

- Replace high-risk cutovers with controlled, observable progressive rollouts  
- Measure real outcomes such as performance, cost, and reliability, not just deployment success  
- Safely test in production with guardrails, governance, and automated rollback  
- Modernize systems incrementally, retiring legacy components and validating new services without disruption  

Whether you're replatforming to the cloud, rebuilding APIs, or evolving a monolith into microservices, this session will give you a practical roadmap to reduce risk while accelerating delivery.

Title: agentctl: A CLI for Managing AI Agents and MCP Servers Like Infrastructure
Abstract: You have `kubectl` for pods, `docker` for containers, and `helm` for charts. But what do you use to register, health-check, start, stop, and discover the growing fleet of AI agents and MCP servers running on your machine and in your cluster? `agentctl` is a zero-dependency Go CLI that treats AI agents and Model Context Protocol servers as first-class managed services. It provides the same operational primitives you expect from infrastructure tooling — registration, lifecycle management, health checks, tagging, auto-discovery, and a live TUI dashboard — applied to the new layer of AI services that's quietly becoming load-bearing in cloud-native stacks. This talk is a live walkthrough of building and using `agentctl`: **Part 1 — The problem (5 min)** You're running Ollama locally, three MCP servers across Cursor and Claude Desktop, a LiteLLM proxy, and two custom agents. They're all managed differently — some are Docker containers, some are Node processes, some are just "I left a terminal tab open." There's no unified view. Health checking is manual. When one crashes overnight you find out the hard way. **Part 2 — Registration and lifecycle (8 min)** We register agents and MCP servers with typed metadata: transport (stdio, SSE, HTTP), confidence thresholds, endpoints, commands, and tags. `agentctl agent start` launches the process, captures the PID, streams logs to `~/.agentctl/logs/`, and tracks status. `agentctl mcp health` probes endpoints or checks PIDs for stdio transports. Everything persists to a single JSON file — no database, no daemon. **Part 3 — Auto-discovery (7 min)** `agentctl discover` scans three sources in parallel: running processes (matching ~30 known signatures including Ollama, LM Studio, CrewAI, LangServe, and any `node` process with "mcp" in its name), well-known ports (11434, 1234, 4000, 3000, etc.), and MCP config files from Claude Desktop, Cursor, and VS Code. New services are registered automatically. Re-runs are idempotent. **Part 4 — The dashboard (5 min)** `agentctl dashboard` renders a live terminal UI refreshing every 3 seconds — agents, MCP servers, recent API calls, health status, confidence scores, all in one view. It's the `htop` for your AI service layer. **Part 5 — Confidence-gated decisions (5 min)** Each agent carries a confidence score (0–1) that maps to a tiered decision framework: green (≥0.85, auto-proceed), yellow (0.60–0.85, notify-proceed), red (<0.60, escalate). We show how this integrates with the broader incident automation platform to gate which actions an agent can take autonomously. **You'll leave with:** - A working pattern for managing AI agents as infrastructure primitives - The auto-discovery approach for finding agents and MCP servers across your environment - A confidence-gating framework applicable to any autonomous agent system - The tool itself — pure Go, zero dependencies, install with `go install`

Title: Responsible Agentic AI for Equitable and Sustainable Cloud Native Telecom
Abstract: Cloud native architectures are redefining telecommunications as 5G evolves toward 6G. Containerized network functions, distributed edge workloads, and AI driven automation now manage spectrum allocation, fault detection, traffic forecasting, and power optimization in real time. As networks become increasingly autonomous and software defined, the responsibility embedded within these intelligent systems becomes a critical cloud native concern. Globally, 4.0 billion people have high speed connectivity, yet 1.5 billion remain under connected and 1.1 billion remain completely unconnected. AI based resource allocation systems, when trained on historically uneven investment patterns, risk reinforcing disparities by prioritizing high revenue urban regions while underserved rural and economically disadvantaged areas receive limited infrastructure upgrades. Within cloud native orchestration layers, such bias can scale rapidly across distributed environments if fairness is not explicitly designed into automation workflows. Environmental impact adds another layer of complexity. Reinforcement learning can dynamically adjust base station power usage to reduce idle energy consumption, and digital twin simulations allow operators to test efficiency strategies before deployment. However, large scale model training and continuous inference across edge and cloud infrastructure require substantial computational resources. The sustainability outcome depends on whether optimization gains exceed the operational footprint of AI driven cloud workloads. This session presents an integrated governance framework that embeds transparency, accountability, fairness evaluation, and carbon reporting directly into AI enabled cloud native network operations. By combining audit mechanisms, allocation assessments, responsibility mapping, and measurable sustainability tracking, telecom providers can align autonomous optimization with equitable access and environmental responsibility. As cloud native networks become increasingly agent driven, responsible AI is essential to ensure scalable innovation delivers measurable societal value alongside performance.
Slides: Bhavika Reddy Jalli - Conf42 Cloud Native 2026.pdf

Title: Cloud-Native Intent Orchestration for Enterprise AI Ecosystems
Abstract: As enterprises embed generative AI into cloud-native platforms, they often face fragmentation—isolated copilots, duplicated capabilities, inconsistent experiences, and governance gaps across distributed systems. This talk presents intent-based orchestration as a cloud-native architectural pattern for unifying enterprise AI into a scalable, composable assistant ecosystem. At the center is an enterprise Agentic AI platform designed with cloud-native principles: modular services, API-driven integration, and elastic scalability. Core components include intent analyzers for interpreting requests, orchestration layers for intelligent task routing, and reasoning engines for context-aware execution. Supporting capabilities such as vector-based knowledge retrieval, dynamic prompt libraries, and state tracking enable reliable, high-quality outputs across environments. The session explores how organizations evolve from passive Q&A bots to agentic workflows capable of multi-step task execution using LLM function calling, prompt chaining, and distributed state management. Real-world examples illustrate cross-domain automation in finance, HR, and IT—while maintaining governance, compliance, and operational observability. Attendees will gain practical guidance on phased cloud-native implementations, integrating AI into microservices architectures, and establishing governance frameworks that align with DevOps and platform engineering practices. We will also examine emerging patterns in AI interoperability and collaborative agent networks. By the end, participants will understand how to architect resilient, interoperable, and scalable AI ecosystems that move beyond isolated tools—transforming enterprise AI into a unified, cloud-native capability.

Title: Reduce AI Agents Costs and Mistakes with Semantic tool Selection
Abstract: AI agents with many tools face a dual problem: they pick the wrong tool and waste tokens because tool descriptions get serialized into the context on every call. As agents scale to 50+ tools, errors increase and costs explode. Semantic tool selection filters tools before they reach the LLM context using vector search, reducing errors and token costs. This talk walks you through building TWO versions of the same travel agent to prove semantic tool selection works. By comparing the agents side-by-side on identical queries, you'll see dramatic improvements in both accuracy and cost and learn how to implement dynamic tool filtering that preserves conversation memory across multi-turn sessions. You'll leave with working Python code and production patterns you can deploy immediately.
Slides: Elizabeth Fuentes Leone - Conf42 Cloud Native 2026.pdf

Title: Engineering Production-Grade Multi-Agent Systems in the Cloud
Abstract: Most organizations begin their AI journey with a single conversational interface. The architectural inflection point occurs when that interface evolves into a distributed, cloud-native system of collaborating agents executing real operational workflows. At this stage, complexity increases in ways that demos rarely expose: orchestration depth grows across services, latency compounds across agent hops, partial failures propagate through reasoning chains, and non-deterministic outputs introduce variability that must be governed rather than ignored. In this talk, I present the evolution of a production order-support platform that transitioned from a monolithic chatbot into a centrally orchestrated, multi-agent architecture deployed in a cloud-native environment. The redesign required explicit coordination control, isolation of failure domains to prevent cascading errors, and observability capable of reconstructing multi-hop reasoning paths across distributed components. The core challenge was not improving prompts, but engineering predictability, resilience, and trust in a system where autonomous agents collaborate. The session examines when orchestration provides advantages over decentralized agent swarms, how well-defined agent boundaries reduce contradictory outputs, and why observability must capture decision artifacts—not just infrastructure metrics. I will also discuss trade-offs between flexibility and determinism, and how governance becomes essential once agents influence production workflows. Attendees will leave with cloud-native architectural patterns and engineering principles for building resilient, traceable, and governable multi-agent systems under real-world workloads.

Title: AI as a Force Multiplier for Cloud Native Enterprise Development
Abstract: Cloud native enterprise systems built on microservices, container orchestration, and API driven platforms have significantly increased the scale and complexity of modern software development. Large organizations now operate hundreds of services, distributed data platforms, and multi cloud CI/CD pipelines. While these architectures enable scalability and rapid innovation, they also introduce substantial cognitive overhead for engineering teams that must navigate evolving service dependencies, documentation ecosystems, compliance requirements, and architectural standards. This session explores how artificial intelligence can act as a practical productivity multiplier within cloud native enterprise development environments. The presentation first examines AI powered knowledge intelligence systems that consolidate fragmented documentation across repositories, architecture diagrams, ticketing systems, and internal knowledge bases. Through semantic search and retrieval augmented generation, developers can query institutional knowledge conversationally and quickly obtain contextual insights from distributed documentation sources. The talk then analyzes AI augmented development workflows where coding assistants support engineers by generating service scaffolding, validating API contracts, recommending refactoring strategies, and identifying potential security vulnerabilities. These capabilities help automate repetitive engineering tasks such as boilerplate code generation, syntax validation, and dependency analysis, allowing developers to focus on system design, reliability engineering, and performance optimization. Finally, the session highlights AI assisted testing and onboarding workflows. Machine learning driven QA platforms can analyze historical defect patterns to generate targeted test scenarios and prioritize high risk modules in CI pipelines, while AI guided onboarding assistants help new engineers interpret complex platform architectures. Together, these capabilities demonstrate how human AI collaboration can accelerate delivery within cloud native engineering teams while preserving strong architectural governance.

Title: Context-Aware AI Development: Unlocking the Next Wave of Engineering Productivity
Abstract: AI-driven development is rapidly shifting from an experimental advantage to a baseline expectation for modern engineering teams. Organizations that delay adopting AI-assisted workflows risk falling behind as productivity standards evolve across the industry. Early evidence from teams already integrating AI into their development processes shows dramatic gains—tasks that previously required weeks of engineering effort can now often be completed in days. Many organizations are now targeting up to 50% productivity improvements by 2026 through the strategic use of AI across coding, testing, documentation, and review workflows. However, a critical limitation remains: context loss. Most AI development tools operate in largely stateless environments, requiring developers to repeatedly re-establish project context—architecture decisions, coding conventions, and historical design choices—with every new interaction. This constant rebuilding of context creates friction and reduces the potential impact of AI collaboration. This talk introduces a practical framework for solving the context problem through context-aware workflows. The approach includes maintaining living project context artifacts, standardizing prompt templates that embed architectural and domain knowledge, and implementing structured session handoff practices that allow AI interactions to persist effectively across development cycles. The session will also explore emerging infrastructure enabling persistent AI collaboration, including new efforts to support stateful AI environments where agents can retain memory, context, and tool-use state across sessions. Attendees will leave with concrete patterns for integrating context-aware AI workflows into their development practices—unlocking the next stage of engineering productivity in the AI-native era.

Title: Cloud-Native AI Infrastructure Transforming Modern Agriculture
Abstract: Agriculture is undergoing a major digital transformation as cloud-native engineering, enterprise automation, and AI-driven infrastructure reshape how food is produced, monitored, and delivered. Modern cloud platforms process large volumes of real-time agricultural data from IoT sensors, drones, and satellite imagery, enabling scalable, resilient data pipelines that support precision farming. This foundation powers capabilities such as soil-health monitoring, targeted irrigation, and intelligent pest management through AI-enabled analytics. Machine learning models trained on multi-season datasets improve crop-yield predictions and enable more informed operational planning across farms and agribusiness ecosystems. Cloud-native automation is also redefining agricultural supply chains through intelligent inventory systems and autonomous machinery that improve efficiency and reliability. Distributed technologies such as blockchain enhance transparency and traceability from cultivation to consumption, strengthening trust while reducing fraud. Deep learning frameworks convert complex, high-dimensional agricultural data into actionable insights that support real-time decision-making. To address labour shortages and operational inefficiencies, robotics and autonomous equipment are increasingly deployed for repetitive or precision-driven tasks. Advanced sensing techniques combined with AI enable rapid detection of pesticide residues and quality anomalies, while IoT-driven irrigation systems optimize water usage to support sustainability goals. Together, cloud-native architectures and AI-powered automation are accelerating the shift toward resilient, scalable, and environmentally conscious agricultural systems capable of meeting long-term global food demands.

Title: Interoperability by Design for Cloud-Native Workflows
Abstract: Cloud-native systems now span containers, Kubernetes clusters, data pipelines, microservices, and multi-cloud infrastructure. With organizations running an average of 2,341 containers and Kubernetes in production for 80% of enterprises, avoiding vendor lock-in and operational fragmentation is no longer optional it is architectural strategy. This session presents a five-plane interoperability framework purpose-built for cloud-native environments: Workflow Definition; Execution & State; Integration; Observability & Lineage; and Packaging & Deployment. The model is grounded in open standards central to the CNCF ecosystem, including CloudEvents, OpenTelemetry, OpenLineage, the OCI Image Specification, and Kubernetes CustomResourceDefinitions. We will review benchmark data demonstrating event-driven systems exceeding 200,000 events per second with median latencies as low as 32 ms in scale-out configurations. OpenTelemetry Collectors have been benchmarked at 15,000 events per second with under 12 ms query latency using 256 MB memory and 15 CPU. Beyond performance, we connect interoperability to measurable reliability outcomes: faster MTTR with mature observability practices, SLO-aware autoscaling that reduces violation duration and cost, and elite DevOps metrics including sub-5% change failure rates and recovery under 60 minutes. Attendees will leave with a contract-first architectural blueprint for decomposing monolithic workflow engines into portable, pluggable components enabling high throughput, low latency, standardized governance, and sustainable reliability at cloud-native scale.

Title: Migration from On-Prem Messaging System to The Cloud: What, How and Why
Abstract: The race to the cloud is on, with enterprises everywhere migrating core infrastructure to stay competitive and cost effective. But when it comes to the messaging systems that power cross-component communications, a simple “lift and shift” isn’t adequate and can be a recipe for failure. The migration path is riddled with complex decisions and design pitfalls unique to every use case. In this session, AWS Cloud Support expert Tom will walk you through the critical stages of rehosting, replatforming, and refactoring, showing you how to unlock maximum performance and reliability for messaging systems. Additionally, Tom will compare traditional message brokers with more modernized serverless messaging services on AWS. By the end of the session, you will have a much more comprehensive understanding of the migration prcess, key questions to ask and some best practices for harnessing the benefits of Cloud.

Title: Database Evolution: Partitioning and Indexing for Cloud-Native Systems
Abstract: The rapid adoption of cloud computing, growing at 39% year-over-year across enterprises, is reshaping how modern databases are designed and operated. This session explores how partitioning and indexing strategies have evolved from rigid, on-premises models to intelligent, adaptive mechanisms built for cloud-native architectures. We’ll examine how modern partitioning approaches dynamically adapt to workload patterns, improving query response times by 47% while reducing administrative overhead by 60%. We will also discuss the transformation of indexing from static B-tree structures to adaptive, multi-dimensional models that reduce storage overhead by 28% and boost query performance by up to 35%. Using benchmarks from frameworks such as OLTP-Bench and iTuned, the session highlights how cloud-native database platforms sustain >99.95% availability, scale elastically to support 10× workload surges within minutes, and process over 10 million transactions per second while lowering operational costs by up to 41% and reducing mean-time-to-resolution by 43% in large deployments. The talk also explores the growing role of machine learning in next-generation database engines. Learned indexes can shrink index size by 10×, adaptive query processors reduce execution time by 45%, and ML-driven workload managers achieve up to 90% prediction accuracy. These capabilities enable self-optimizing, resilient, and cost-efficient database systems while improving developer productivity by up to 50% and reducing DBA workload by 60%.

Title: Design Systems for Scalable Cloud-Native Analytics Platforms
Abstract: Cloud-native analytics platforms must deliver consistency, accessibility, and performance across distributed teams, services, and interfaces. This session explores the architectural foundations required to build scalable design systems in modern cloud-native environments. It focuses on five measurable pillars: component modularity, token-driven theming, cross-platform consistency, versioning discipline, and governance maturity. We begin with component modularity, showing how decoupled UI primitives enable independent deployment, reduce duplication, and accelerate delivery across microservices-driven teams. The session then examines token-based theming, where design tokens act as a single source of truth for visual and interaction properties, enabling consistent updates across dynamically deployed applications. Next, we address cross-platform consistency, outlining patterns that ensure cohesive experiences across web dashboards, embedded analytics, mobile applications, and operational tools in distributed ecosystems. Attendees will learn how to maintain parity while supporting product-level flexibility. We also cover versioning strategies critical in cloud-native systems, including semantic versioning, release channels, and backward compatibility approaches that prevent breaking dependent services. Finally, we explore governance models that scale with organizational complexity, including contribution workflows, accessibility standards, and decision frameworks that balance autonomy with alignment.

Title: Autonomous Delivery and Multi-Modal Fulfillment in Cloud Native Commerce
Abstract: Last-mile delivery remains one of the most complex and cost intensive layers of modern retail infrastructure. As same-day and sub-hour fulfillment become baseline expectations, traditional driver-based models struggle to scale efficiently in dense urban environments. Retailers are now turning to autonomous delivery systems to modernize fulfillment architecture and improve reliability, cost control, and operational elasticity. This session explores three primary autonomous delivery modalities reshaping enterprise fulfillment: sidewalk delivery robots, unmanned aerial drones, and autonomous middle-mile trucking systems. Sidewalk robots operate on pedestrian infrastructure using zero-emission electric systems. Drones enable direct point-to-point routing that bypasses surface congestion. Autonomous trucks are among the most mature applications, particularly for predictable warehouse-to-store middle-mile routes. Real-world deployments show this shift is already active. Walmart and Gatik operate fully driverless middle-mile routes in Arkansas without safety drivers. Walmart and Wing conduct drone delivery operations in the Dallas metropolitan area targeting fulfillment within 30 minutes of order placement. 7-Eleven and Nuro have piloted autonomous suburban delivery programs. The session also examines emerging multi-modal coordination models, including robot-to-drone handoff systems piloted in Dallas through Serve Robotics and Wing. These integrated transfer and scheduling frameworks illustrate how cloud native orchestration, distributed systems, and real-time coordination enable scalable autonomous logistics. Attendees will leave with a practical framework for evaluating adoption barriers such as regulatory fragmentation, infrastructure constraints, and environmental limitations, and how cloud native architecture supports resilient, scalable autonomous fulfillment networks.
Slides: Cijin Lonappan Kappani - Conf42 Cloud Native 2026.pdf

Title: Event-Driven Validation for Cloud-Native Resilience
Abstract: Modern healthcare platforms run on complex cloud-native ecosystems composed of microservices, APIs, containers, and distributed data pipelines. In such environments, reliability directly impacts operational continuity and regulatory compliance. Traditional validation approaches, manual, periodic, and reactive, cannot scale with the velocity and interdependencies of Kubernetes-based systems. This session introduces SupportPlus, an event-driven automation framework designed to embed continuous validation into cloud-native architectures. Built on microservices, serverless components, and Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), the platform ingests high-volume event streams and executes parallel validation workflows across services, APIs, and data layers. Using metadata-driven rules and distributed orchestration patterns, the framework enables real-time detection, automated remediation, and observable control loops within containerized environments. Enterprise-scale deployment demonstrates measurable improvements in system availability, incident resolution time, and operational efficiency, while automated compliance and security checks convert audit readiness into a continuous byproduct of the platform. By unifying validation, monitoring, and remediation within a Kubernetes-native, event-driven architecture, this approach provides a scalable blueprint for engineering resilient, self-healing cloud-native systems in regulated and mission-critical environments.

Title: Keep Calm and Run on AWS: Engineering Compliance Without Abandoning Your Stack
Abstract:
Your datacenter vendor says you need on-premises infrastructure. The sovereignty crowd says you need seven EU-native providers. Your legal team forwards another GDPR article at 9am. Customers are refusing after reading biased articles in the newspaper paid by the local DC owner. Your engineers just want to ship.

This session is a practitioner's defence of running AWS in regulated European environments — what actually needs solving, what is compliance theater, and how to build a stack your engineering team can still operate at 3am. We'll cover what Outposts genuinely solves and where it quietly fails — control plane metadata, backup destinations, and the DR trap nobody puts in the sales deck. We'll look at real capacity planning and benchmarking: what the performance delta actually looks like and what causes it. And we'll make the case against trading a single well-governed AWS account for seven EU vendors with seven SLAs, seven blast radiuses, and seven on-call rotations. Finally — AWS European Sovereign Cloud launched in January 2026. We'll cut through the noise on CLOUD Act, DORA, and NIS2 to show where ESC moves the needle and where the legal gaps remain.

Sovereignty without operational maturity is just compliance theater. Let's talk about what real compliance engineering looks like.

Title: Cloud-Native Data Lifecycle Governance for Trusted AI & BI
Abstract: As AI-powered Business Intelligence (BI) accelerates cloud adoption, organizations face increasing pressure to implement end-to-end data governance across distributed, hybrid, and multi-cloud environments. In cloud-native architectures, governance can no longer be an afterthought—it must be embedded into the data lifecycle itself. This session presents a practical, research-backed blueprint for implementing cloud-native data lifecycle governance—from secure ingestion to auditable deletion—while maintaining performance and scalability. Drawing from real-world implementations, we explore how advanced lifecycle governance improves operational efficiency and reduces compliance risk. Attendees will learn how to integrate clear data ownership models, AI-powered classification, role-based access control (RBAC), and metadata-driven catalogs into cloud-native platforms. We’ll examine secure ingestion patterns and self-organizing frameworks that reduce latency, along with AI-enhanced validation techniques that strengthen anomaly detection. The session also covers tiered storage architectures and dynamic allocation strategies that balance cost, reliability, and compliance requirements. Finally, we explore automated retention and audit mechanisms that accelerate regulatory response times and enable consistent, policy-driven governance across cloud environments.

Title: Designing Compliant Digital Onboarding: Cloud-Native Architectures for Trustworthy, Audit-Ready Financial Services
Abstract: Digital onboarding is among the most compliance-sensitive workflows in customer-facing financial systems. It establishes legal relationships, defines consent boundaries, and determines access to regulated services — making interaction integrity not just a UX consideration, but a core architectural requirement in cloud-native environments. This session explores how consent, disclosures, and identity verification can be designed as structured, evidence-producing interactions rather than isolated interface steps. In cloud-native systems — where services are distributed, independently deployable, and composed dynamically — ensuring that onboarding outcomes remain consistent, traceable, and defensible demands deliberate design at the architecture level. The presentation examines how disclosures and consent function as formal compliance artifacts. Designing these interactions with versioning and traceability — aligned to cloud-native principles of immutability and observability — ensures that onboarding records remain defensible during audits or disputes while maintaining clarity and trust for customers. The session also addresses identity and access design using progressive assurance models, in which higher-risk actions are enabled only as confidence in identity increases. In microservices-based onboarding flows, this approach requires that access decisions, identity state, and data-handling boundaries are consistently communicated and enforced across service boundaries — balancing risk management with a coherent user experience. Finally, the talk examines exception handling and continuity in distributed onboarding workflows. Interruptions, retries, and partial completions are common in real-world scenarios, and poorly designed exception paths — especially across loosely coupled cloud services — often create compliance gaps. Designing for interaction integrity ensures onboarding outcomes remain explainable and defensible even when flows do not complete as intended.

Title: AI-Augmented Azure Ops: Copilot for Intelligent Cloud Resource Management
Abstract: Cloud native environments demand rapid diagnostics, scalable operations, and continuous optimization across complex infrastructures. Copilot in Azure represents a new approach to cloud operations by combining artificial intelligence with human expertise to improve how teams manage and troubleshoot Azure environments. Integrated with the Azure control plane and environment telemetry, Copilot allows users to interact with their cloud resources using natural language. Instead of manually navigating hundreds of Azure services and thousands of resource types, teams can query configurations, analyze performance metrics, and understand dependencies through conversational interaction. This capability simplifies day-to-day operations while helping engineers quickly identify issues and optimize system performance. Behind this experience is an architecture that combines large language models with real time telemetry and configuration data. By grounding AI responses in actual environment context, Copilot delivers insights that support more informed decision making and faster diagnostics across cloud and edge systems. Human expertise remains essential to this process. Engineers and technical advisors contribute by validating telemetry mappings, reviewing system design considerations, and developing internal troubleshooting guidance. Their oversight helps ensure that AI generated insights remain reliable, transparent, and aligned with operational best practices. This session explores how Copilot in Azure enhances cloud native operations through natural language interfaces, contextual diagnostics, and collaborative AI systems. Attendees will gain practical insight into how AI assisted tooling can simplify Azure resource management while strengthening governance, reliability, and operational efficiency in modern cloud environments.
Slides: Sampath Rao Madarapu - Conf42 Cloud Native 2026.pdf

Title: Beyond Dashboards: Deep-Dive Network Observability with eBPF
Abstract: Why do dashboards show "green" while users experience failures? Because traditional monitoring tells you when a system is broken, but it rarely tells you why. In this session, I break down the paradigm shift from legacy monitoring to true observability, specifically focusing on the most elusive layer: the network. I’ll dive into the "black box" of Kubernetes networking, explaining why traditional iptables and sidecar-based approaches often struggle to capture the full picture. I’ll introduce eBPF as the game-changing alternative, demonstrating how it operates as a kernel-level superpower that bypasses legacy bottlenecks. You will learn how eBPF-based agents deployed as DaemonSets, can provide granular, real-time visibility into every packet traversing your cluster. Finally, I’ll walk through an open-source observability toolkit you can leverage today to map Pod-to-Pod and Pod-to-Node traffic. I’ll share a practical blueprint for building a network-aware platform that makes your infrastructure transparent, scalable, and most importantly easy to debug.

Title: Cloud-Native Friendly Fraud Detection with Temporal Graph Attention and Transformers
Abstract: First-party (friendly) fraud is one of the fastest-growing threats in payments, with industry estimates attributing 60–75% of chargeback volume to friendly fraud and $48B+ in annual global losses. Unlike third-party fraud, the transaction is often legitimate at purchase time, while fraud occurs later through dispute filing. This temporal disconnect limits point-of-transaction controls and helps explain why rule-based systems typically capture only 23–31% of confirmed friendly fraud cases. Traditional machine learning improves detection to approximately 38–47%, but remains constrained by feature engineering and limited ability to model networked behavior across accounts, devices, merchants, and time. This talk proposes an attention-driven detection framework that combines heterogeneous temporal transaction graphs with transformer-based behavioral sequence modeling. Payment ecosystems are represented as large-scale graphs spanning cardholders, transactions, merchants, devices, IPs, and addresses, at issuer-scale volumes: 50–100M accounts, 15–25M merchants, and graphs reaching 10–50B edges, with 100–500M edges added daily. Graph attention highlights suspicious structural relationships (such as device-sharing or repeated cross-merchant patterns), while transformer self-attention captures sequential signals such as dispute targeting behavior across time windows. The session outlines how these architectures can be evaluated using fraud detection metrics such as precision, recall, AUC, and cost-sensitive threshold optimization, benchmarked against rule-based and traditional ML baselines. It also discusses deployment considerations relevant to cloud-native systems, including scalable graph construction, segment-level calibration, and human-in-the-loop review workflows to reduce false positives and prevent customer friction in real-world payment environments.

Title: Behavioral Analytics for Real-Time Fraud Prevention
Abstract: Traditional fraud detection systems rely heavily on static rules and thresholds, making it difficult to detect sophisticated or evolving threats. Behavioral analytics introduces a more adaptive approach by building dynamic user profiles from signals such as navigation behavior, keystroke patterns, device fingerprints, and transaction activity. By analyzing these behavioral signatures in real time, modern fraud detection platforms can identify anomalies earlier and with greater accuracy. Machine learning–driven anomaly detection continuously refines behavioral baselines, helping organizations distinguish between legitimate user behavior changes and potential fraud attempts. Recent implementations show significant improvements, including higher detection rates, reduced false alerts, and faster response times. When combined with contextual intelligence—such as device data, location patterns, and environmental signals—these systems provide strong security while maintaining a seamless user experience through adaptive authentication. This session explores how behavioral biometrics, contextual analytics, and machine learning can be integrated to build scalable, real-time fraud prevention frameworks. Attendees will gain insights into designing adaptive fraud detection systems that improve security, reduce operational costs, and enhance customer experience.
Slides: Sharath Reddy Polu - Conf42 Cloud Native 2026.pdf

Title: Security in Your Pocket: AI Risk Assessment for Cloud-Native SMBs
Abstract: As small and home-based businesses adopt cloud-native platforms—e-commerce SaaS, APIs, serverless backends, cloud services, and social media integrations—their digital footprint becomes distributed, dynamic, and difficult to secure. Unlike enterprises, these organizations lack security teams and DevSecOps pipelines. Enterprise security platforms assume complex ownership models, while consumer tools overlook business-level risk. This creates a widening cloud-native security gap. This talk presents Security in Your Pocket, an AI-driven, self-service cybersecurity framework implemented through the securemystore.ai platform for SKRM Technologies. The framework addresses four systemic gaps: limited cloud asset visibility, absence of clear security ownership, uncontrolled third-party and AI tool exposure, and misalignment between traditional security solutions and small business realities. Modern small businesses operate across constantly changing cloud assets—websites, APIs, SaaS tools, e-commerce platforms, and cloud services—many of which fall outside traditional security monitoring. Our six-layer architecture integrates asset discovery, vulnerability detection, continuous monitoring, AI correlation and intelligence, business-aligned risk scoring, and continuous attestation. AI models correlate risks across distributed assets and translate technical findings into plain-language remediation guidance. Evaluation in simulated small business cloud environments identified misconfigurations, weak authentication, and exposed services with minimal false positives. Results show improved visibility, faster prioritization, and actionable protection without requiring dedicated security expertise. This session demonstrates how AI-enabled human–AI collaboration can operationalize continuous cloud-native security as an accessible service—bringing automated risk intelligence directly into the hands of small digital businesses.

Title: Cloud MCP Servers Under Fire: Security Risks, Vulnerability Patterns, and Practical Defenses
Abstract: As MCP servers become a new integration layer between AI systems, tools, and cloud services, they also create a new and often underestimated attack surface. Misplaced trust boundaries, excessive privileges, weak isolation, insecure tool exposure, and vulnerable connector logic can quickly turn MCP infrastructure into a high-impact security problem. In this talk, I will explore the main security risks associated with cloud-based MCP servers, propose a practical classification of vulnerability patterns, and show how these issues can affect real-world AI-enabled environments. I will also share actionable recommendations for building and operating MCP services more securely, including architecture choices, access control principles, validation strategies, and defensive monitoring approaches. The session is aimed at security engineers, cloud architects, AI platform teams, and anyone designing AI-connected cloud systems who wants to understand how to reduce risk before MCP becomes the next major blind spot.

Title: Azure Attacks Unpacked: Real-World Threats and Lessons Learned
Abstract: Azure attacks evolved beyond passwords. Attackers exploit hybrid identities, abuse trusted relationships, and move with alarming sophistication. See real TTPs from Microsoft's Digital Defense Report report via quick demos, then learn strategies to protect your environment and educate users.

Title: Secure your cloud automation
Abstract: So you decided to use PowerShell and other tools to automate tasks in your cloud platform? Are you sure this automation is secured properly and not causing any more attack surfaces for malicious actors? In this talk I will cover different ways of automating your cloud platform and risks involved with these techniques. I will go over topics like authentication, secrets, auditability and more. I’ll also show situation I encountered in the field and how these were fixed to provide more confidentiality, integrity and availability. So if you want to learn to automate your could platform with a zero trust policy, then join this talk! Afterward you will know common mistakes and how to avoid them while also being capable of setting up proper automation in the cloud!

Title: Post-Quantum Cryptography: Securing Multi-Cloud APIs Against Quantum Threats
Abstract: The emergence of cryptographically relevant quantum computers poses an existential threat to cloud-native API architectures that depend on RSA and Elliptic Curve Cryptography for securing data-in-transit and establishing trust. Quantum algorithms capable of solving discrete logarithm and integer factorization problems will render current public-key infrastructure obsolete, exposing organizations to harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks where adversaries collect encrypted data today for future decryption. This session shares practical lessons from assessing and mitigating quantum risk across distributed API ecosystems on heterogeneous cloud platforms. A hands-on risk assessment framework will be presented covering cryptographic asset discovery, data classification based on confidentiality timelines, and API criticality evaluation. The session examines standardized post-quantum algorithms, including ML-KEM (Kyber) for key encapsulation and ML-DSA (Dilithium) for digital signatures, with honest comparisons of their security properties, performance trade-offs, and real-world deployment considerations against classical alternatives. Mitigation centers on hybrid cryptographic implementations combining classical and post-quantum algorithms within TLS, providing defense-in-depth during the transition period while maintaining cryptographic agility. Implementation challenges are examined through concrete examples, including public key size increases (3-10x larger than ECC), latency overhead (10-30%), and client ecosystem heterogeneity spanning modern cloud runtimes to legacy systems. Attendees will leave with practical guidance on building phased migration roadmaps, prioritizing high-risk components, and navigating the 24-36 month timeline for deploying quantum-resistant infrastructure across multi-cloud environments.
Slides: Rajasingh Gandhi Ramdas - Conf42 Cloud Native 2026.pdf

Title: Stablecoins as Cloud-Native Payment Infrastructure
Abstract:
Cloud architects face a new reality: payment infrastructure is decentralizing. Stablecoins solve cross-border friction at internet speed, but require production-grade reliability - think CloudWatch for onchain reserves, Kubernetes for validator fleets, Terraform for compliance configs. Stablecoins represent the first cloud-native payment infrastructure - programmable, global, 24/7 settlement without intermediaries. This talk dissects their architecture through real-world deployments: Polygon's scaling to 1B+ tx, El Salvador's Chivo Wallet (national Bitcoin rollout).  I'll cover:

- Technical stack: Onchain observability, reserve monitoring, real-time compliance
- Custody & settlement: Multi-sig, institutional-grade rails vs traditional banking
- Scaling patterns: From testnet to production (Polygon's infra evolution)
- Cloud integration: API layers, event streaming, hybrid custody models

The talk bridges Web3 realities with cloud-native thinking: what breaks at scale, how to monitor programmable money, and why traditional payment processors can't compete on latency/cost. Attendees leave with concrete patterns for building/maintaining stablecoin infrastructure in hybrid cloud environments.

Title: Building a Production Computer Vision Verification System for Warehouse Receiving
Abstract: I will present a case study of designing and deploying a computer vision verification system for warehouse receiving in an industrial supply chain. The session focuses not only on detecting visually similar spare parts, but also on how the solution was embedded into an existing operational workflow under real production constraints. It covers the end-to-end design of an AI-assisted receiving process, including image-based verification, the use of catalog and technical reference data, and integration with warehouse operations. Special attention is given to confidence thresholds, exception handling, human-in-the-loop validation, service reliability, and the trade-offs between accuracy and throughput in a time-sensitive environment. Attendees will also see how the system was operated in practice, how user interaction was designed for warehouse personnel, and how the deployed solution improved receiving quality and operational consistency while reducing spare-parts mismatches by 99%.
